overview.title: How to read Genesis
overview.content:
If you’re like most people, you’ve wondered about the deep questions of life: Why are we here? What is life all about? Genesis takes you back to the beginning of time to find the answers. It tells about many beginnings: the first plants and animals; the first man and woman; the first sin; the first news of God’s salvation. It also shows God’s dealings with Noah, Abraham, Joseph, and others, repeatedly “demonstrating his desire to have fellowship with his people. 

Genesis is an exciting book, full of unforgettable stories that introduce fascinating people and detail remarkable events. It is an important book to read, for it is the foundation of all that follows in the pages of Scripture. In the first 11 chapters of Genesis you’ll get a big-picture view, covering four major events: the creation, the fall of humanity, the flood, and the record of nations that came into existence afterward. In the next 39 chapters (12-50) you’ll get a close-up view of one family—Abraham, Isaac, Jacob/Israel and his descendants. God called this family to himself so that through them he might eventually bless all the families of the earth (12:2).

As you read Genesis, take special notice of the focus Genesis places on the relationship between God and humanity—broken in the garden and restored through sacrifices and personal encounters with God. Through the stories of history Genesis illustrates cycles of sin and repentance—and continually shows God at work to accomplish his plans.

overview.title: Who wrote this book and when?
overview.content: Moses wrote it around 1446–1406 bc. He relied on revelation from God and, perhaps, earlier oral or written records.

overview.title: To whom was it written?
overview.content: Because it announces that "all the families on earth will be blessed" (12:3), it seems fair to conclude that Moses believed all people could benefit from its message.

overview.title: Why was it written?
overview.content: To show that when God made the creation, it was good. Genesis goes on to say that when sin entered the world, it corrupted the creation and led God to initiate his plan for salvation.

overview.title: What period of history does it cover?
overview.content: From the time of creation (a date that cannot be known for sure) to the time when the Israelites arrived in Egypt and grew into a nation (about 1800 bc).

overview.title: SourceView Insights.
overview.content:
Reading Genesis in SourceView format will help you discover some new insights into this book. Consider, for example, the 100 times God speaks: 38 of these occurrences appear in the first 11 chapters which focus on the events from creation through Babel; 44 are found in chapters 12 to 23 which focus on the life of Abraham; and the remainder—18 occurrences—are located in those chapters which deal with Isaac, Jacob/Israel, and Joseph (chapter 24 to the end). The emphasis is clearly on God’s words to Abra“ham. Though more than half of the book is dedicated to the affairs of Abraham’s descendants, less than one-fifth of God’s spoken communication occurs in this part of the book.

God speaks the most to Abraham (1,138 words; see God 39-48,53-62,66-72,75,77-82); second most to Noah (648 words; see God 29-36); and third most to Jacob/Israel (320 words; see God 87-88,90-100). He also speaks to at least a dozen other individuals including four women, a murderer, a slave, and a pagan king. Can you discover who they all are?

In addition to God there are another 61 individuals or groups who form the speaking cast of this very dramatic book! The four leading roles (represented in green) are Abraham, Isaac, Israel (Jacob), and Joseph. A focus on the longest speaking part of each of these reveals some very interesting insights. Each of the first three reserve their longest speech for the end of their life to address issues about their children and future generations (see Abraham 38, Isaac 27, and Jacob/Israel 82). In contrast, Joseph’s longest speech comes early in his life and has to do with the interpretation of Pharaoh’s dream (Joseph 19).

Among the supporting cast (in blue), some of the larger speeches that stand out are:
• the faith-filled testimony of a nameless slave (see Abraham’s Servant 11—461 words),
• the self-sacrificing commitment of a repentant man (see Judah 13—395 words),
• the desperate cry of a pagan king for revelation (see Pharaoh (Joseph’s) 3—155 words), and,
• the anguished report of men trapped by their past (see Joseph’s Brothers 14—126 words).
